question: Does sustained activity degrade quality within a session (empathy fatigue)?
method: Within-session commit pattern analysis: split each session into quartiles by commit position, measure repair rate, feature rate, and lesson production per quartile. Paired t-tests (first half vs second half) for statistical significance.

session_length_correlation.interpretation: Session length does NOT predict repair rate (rho near zero). Length is not a fatigue proxy.

verdict: PARTIAL — empathy fatigue (repair quality degradation) NOT DETECTED. Creative fatigue (feature/lesson production decline) CONFIRMED at medium-large effect size (d=-0.66 for features, d=-0.41 for lessons). The swarm does not break more as sessions progress — it creates less.
mechanism: Diminishing returns, not integral windup (ISO-13). Context accumulation shifts work from novel production toward maintenance/handoff. This is structural (sessions end with handoff by convention) but the Q2→Q3 transition (before handoff) shows the effect is real, not just an artifact.
confounders: Sessions end with handoff/maint by convention — inflates Q4 maint rate, but Q2→Q3 transition is pre-handoff
Concurrency (N) varies within session — not controlled
Commit classification is keyword-based, not semantic
git log -500 samples recent sessions disproportionately
falsification_check: If quality remains flat regardless of helper load → empathy fatigue is not a swarm phenomenon. RESULT: repair quality IS flat (d=0.14, not significant). Creative output declines. Partial falsification.
prescriptions: Feature-first ordering: schedule novel experiments early in session, defer maintenance to end
Mid-session compaction after 6th commit to reset context accumulation
Track within-session production curve as orient.py metric
next_tests: Control for concurrency (N) — does fatigue differ at N=1 vs N≥3?
Measure Sharpe quality per quartile (not just counts)
Test whether mid-session compaction actually resets the curve
